title: The 'mystical' foundation of democratic society, mythmaking and truth in The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (John Ford 1962)
author: Ungureanu

opening_move.description: The paper opens with the foundational paradox in democratic theory: how can a people legitimately found a constitutional order if no already-legitimate authority exists at the founding moment? This is the pouvoir constituant problem framed as an aporia that cannot be resolved without circularity.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding this aporia is necessary to evaluate whether democratic legitimacy can be sustained without either naive teleology or nihilistic deconstruction

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper stages a contrast between Habermasian progressive reconciliation (teleological, smuggles convergence) and Derridean deconstruction (hyperbolic aporia, loses practical traction), then proposes a critical-Enlightenment middle way that retains both aporias and the possibility of non-teleological progress

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Hermeneutic-political reading of a film as a philosophical parable; close attention to plot structure, visual composition (Pompey classroom scene), and narrative voice as carriers of political argument
epistemic_mode.notes: The film is treated as a site of theoretical thinking, not as an empirical object. The case does not test the framework — it instantiates and generates it. This is interpretive constitution of the argument, not empirical support.

case_type.case_name: The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (John Ford, 1962)

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory — legitimacy, founding violence, truth and myth

confidence_notes: High confidence overall. The move sequence is unusually clear due to the paper's explicit section structure. The main classification decision is whether the film functions as illustrative or constitutive evidence — I classify it as constitutive because the paper's positive normative claims (that democracy requires truth-telling, that myths can be pathological) are derived from the film reading rather than established prior to it and then illustrated.
